text: Laparoscopic repair of left periduodenal hernia, a video presentation from the Mount Sinai Hospital. Patient is a twenty-six-year-old male with no past medical or surgical history who has been experiencing episodic abdominal pain every five years for the last fifteen years. He had two episodes in the last five months which required emergency room visits.

Upper GI series showed a cluster of jejunum just left of the duodenum in the left upper quadrant, consistent with the left paraduodenal hernia. CT scan showed an encapsulated circumscribed mass of the small bowel in the left upper quadrant, also consistent with a left periduodenal hernia. Periduodenal hernias can be right or left-sided. Right-sided periduodenal hernias are characterized by small bowel herniating posterior to the superior mesenteric vessels through the fossa of wall dire. Jejunum is typically trapped in the right upper quadrant behind the right mesocolon and mesentery of the cecum. In left peridodinal hernias, bowel passes through the fossa of Lanzerz. Small bowel is typically trapped between the left mesocolon and the posterior abdominal wall. The free edge of the left mesocolon contains the inferior mesenteric vein as well as the ascending left colic artery.
The surgeon enters the abdomen through an incision in the left subcostal region using an optical viewing technique, with six 5mm ports placed for laparoscopic access.

The surgeon identifies the cecum and terminal ileum in the right lower quadrant and traces the small bowel retrograde until encountering abnormal adhesions between loops of small bowel and the mesentery. The small bowel loop is reflected towards the right abdomen, revealing an abnormal fusion of the left mesocolon and small bowel mesentery. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques are employed to separate the left mesocolon from the small bowel mesentery.

The surgeon lyses adhesions to reveal the fossa of Lanzert, exposing the hernia sac containing multiple loops of small bowel. The loops are carefully reduced from the hernia sac, with the fossa located left of the fourth portion of the duodenum and posterior to the left mesocolon. The edge of the left mesocolon is visible, containing the inferior mesenteric vein. After reduction, the surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz by running the small bowel retrograde.

The surgeon begins the repair of the hernia by closing the hernia orifice in two layers. The first layer involves a purse string silk suture that approximates the small bowel mesentery, proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon. The second layer uses a running silk suture to reinforce the approximation of the proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon.
The surgeon completes the hernia repair procedure by applying fibrin sealant over the closed hernia site to reinforce the closure.

Paraduodenal hernias result from congenital malformations forming potential spaces near ligament of trites. They accounted for thirty to fifty percent of internal hernias before gastric bypasses were common. Most patients present in the fourth to sixth decade of life with obstructive or ischemic bowel symptoms. Diagnoses can be made on CT scan or upper GI series. In left paraduodenal hernia, CT and upper GI series show loops of jejunum in the left upper quadrant, lateral to the fourth portion of duodenum. Right paraduodenal hernias on CT demonstrate loops of jejunum in the right upper quadrant behind the mesocolon. Treatment for both types is surgery. Approach can be laparoscopic or open. Important steps of the operation include lysis of the adhesions, reduction of hernia, bowel resection if necessary, and closure of the defect.

In summary, periduodenal hernias are congenital defects near the ligament trites resulting from malrotation. Left periduodenal hernias occur through the fossa of Lanzert. Right periduodenal hernias occur through the fossa of Waldeyer. Diagnosis can be made with CT or upper GI series. Treatment can be performed through laparoscopic surgical reduction of bowel and primary closure of the defect.
